fixes.napkin_math: Harvest: 100 µW continuous. Supercap: assume 0.1F charged to 3.3V. Stored energy = 0.5 × 0.1 × 3.3² = 0.545 J. Usable energy (discharge to 2.5V): 0.5 × 0.1 × (3.3² − 2.5²) = 0.232 J. MCU active draw: 30 mW. Energy per inference burst (20ms active): 30 mW × 20 ms = 0.6 mJ. Bursts per supercap discharge: 232 mJ / 0.6 mJ ≈ 386 bursts. Charge time from empty to 3.3V: E/P = 0.545 J / 100 µW ≈ 5,450 s ≈ 91 min. Practical duty cycle: charge for 91 min, then run ~386 inferences in rapid succession (386 × 20 ms = 7.7 s burst window), then recharge. Effective rate: 386 inferences / 91 min ≈ **4.2 inferences per minute**. For structural health monitoring (detecting bearing degradation over weeks), this is more than sufficient.

Solar panel: 50mm × 50mm = 25 cm². Sunny harvest (~20 mW/cm² × 20% efficiency): ~100 mW. Cloudy: ~10 mW (10× less). Supercap energy: 0.5 × 0.47F × 3.3² = 2.56 J = 0.71 mWh. Inference energy: assume 25 mJ per inference on Cortex-M7 + quiescent 10 µW. Sunny: harvest 100 mW, consumption per inference = 25 mJ. Max rate = 100 mW / 25 mW = 4 inferences/s with energy to spare. Supercap stays full. Cloudy: harvest 10 mW. Max sustainable rate = 10 mW / 25 mJ = 0.4 inferences/s. If we try 1 inf/s (25 mW consumption), net drain = 25 − 10 = 15 mW. Supercap drain time = 2.56 J / 0.015 W = 170 s ≈ 2.8 min. Strategy: on cloudy days, reduce to 1 inference every 2.5 s (= 10 mW average) to balance harvest rate. **Sunny: ~4 inf/s. Cloudy: ~0.4 inf/s.** Adaptive duty cycling based on supercap voltage is essential.
fixes.realistic_solution: Build the energy budget from physics. Sunny: ~100 mW harvest from 25 cm² panel, sustains ~4 inferences/s at 25 mJ each — supercap stays full. Cloudy: ~10 mW harvest, net drain if running at full rate. Supercap (0.71 mWh) drains in ~170 s at 15 mW net deficit. Solution: monitor supercap voltage and adaptively reduce inference rate. At 10 mW harvest, sustainable rate is ~0.4 inf/s. Implement a voltage-triggered duty cycle: full rate above 3.0V, reduced rate below 2.8V, sleep below 2.5V. This ensures the device never fully depletes and can resume when sun returns.

fixes.napkin_math: Average power = (P_active × t_active + P_sleep × t_sleep) / t_period. Period: t_period = 100 ms. Inference: t_active = 20 ms at P_active = 50 mW. Sleep: t_sleep = 80 ms at P_sleep = 10 µW. Energy per period = (50 mW × 20 ms) + (10 µW × 80 ms) = 1,000 µJ + 0.8 µJ ≈ 1,000.8 µJ = 1.0 mJ. Average power = 1.0 mJ / 100 ms = **10 mW** — 10× over the 1 mW budget. To meet 1 mW: need energy per period ≤ 100 µJ. At 50 mW active, max t_active = 100 µJ / 50 mW = 2 ms per 100 ms period (2% duty cycle). This means either: (a) reduce inference to 2 ms (need model optimization or faster MCU), or (b) run inference less frequently — e.g., once per second with 20 ms active: average = 1 mJ / 1000 ms = 1 mW. DMA is critical: it transfers 3.2 KB audio chunks (0.1 s × 16 kHz × 2 bytes) from ADC to SRAM while CPU sleeps, ensuring t_active is purely inference time. Without DMA, CPU must poll/copy data while awake, increasing active time and blowing the power budget.

A typical TinyML device has ~256 KB of SRAM. A mobile phone has ~8 GB of RAM. Ratio: 8 GB / 256 KB = (8 × 1,073,741,824) / (256 × 1,024) = 8,589,934,592 / 262,144 = **32,768×**. A phone has over 32,000× more RAM than a microcontroller. This illustrates the massive resource gap: a single INT8 activation tensor of 256 × 256 × 16 = 1 MB already exceeds the entire MCU SRAM budget.

Vector addition C[i] = A[i] + B[i]: 1 FLOP per element, 12 bytes moved (read A, read B, write C, each 4 bytes for FP32). Arithmetic Intensity = 1 FLOP / 12 bytes = 0.083 Ops/Byte. Cortex-M4 Ridge Point: Peak compute ~336 MFLOPS. SRAM bandwidth depends on bus width and clock; at 168 MHz with 32-bit bus: ~672 MB/s (single-cycle access). Ridge Point = 336 MFLOPS / 672 MB/s ≈ 0.5 Ops/Byte. Since vector-add AI (0.083) << Ridge Point (0.5), the operation is **memory-bound** — the CPU finishes computation faster than data can be fed to it. This is characteristic of element-wise operations on MCUs. Convolutions with higher AI (e.g., 3×3 conv ≈ 4.5 Ops/Byte) can approach or exceed the ridge point.
fixes.realistic_solution: The operation is definitively memory-bound. A vector addition C[i] = A[i] + B[i] performs 1 FLOP per element while moving 12 bytes (two 4-byte reads + one 4-byte write). Arithmetic Intensity = 1/12 ≈ 0.083 Ops/Byte. The Cortex-M4's Ridge Point (ratio of peak compute to peak memory bandwidth) is approximately 0.5 Ops/Byte, assuming 336 MFLOPS compute and ~672 MB/s SRAM bandwidth at 168 MHz. Since 0.083 << 0.5, the vector-add is firmly in the memory-bound regime. The CPU spends most of its time waiting for data, not computing.

fixes.napkin_math: Cortex-M4 at 168 MHz delivers ~336 MFLOPS (2 FLOPS/cycle via single-cycle MAC). Model requires 70 MFLOPs. Inference time = 70 MFLOP / 336 MFLOPS = **0.208 s = 208 ms**. Deadline: 1000 ms (must finish before next audio chunk). 208 ms << 1000 ms → **deadline met** with 792 ms of slack. The MCU can sleep for ~79% of each cycle, or the slack can absorb interrupt overhead, feature extraction (e.g., MFCC computation), and I/O. Note: if we used a sliding window with 250 ms stride, the 208 ms inference would barely fit (42 ms slack), making optimization more critical.

**Strategy A: Centralized OTA update.** Download new model: 1 MB over BLE at 1 Mbps = 8 s at 40 mW radio = 320 mJ. Flash write: 1 MB at ~5 mJ/KB = 5,000 mJ. Total per device: ~5,320 mJ. Fleet (100K devices): 532 kJ. **Strategy B: Federated Learning.** Local training: 10 epochs × 1,000 samples × 0.5 mJ/sample = 5,000 mJ compute. Upload gradients: 100 KB at 1 Mbps = 0.8 s at 40 mW = 32 mJ. Download aggregated model: 100 KB = 32 mJ. Flash write: 100 KB at 5 mJ/KB = 500 mJ. Total per device: ~5,564 mJ. Fleet: 556 kJ. **Comparison:** Strategy A ≈ 5.3 J/device. Strategy B ≈ 5.6 J/device. FL is ~5% more expensive per round despite 10× less data transfer, because on-device training energy dominates. The FL advantage emerges only if you need many rounds of centralized OTA (each 5.3 J) vs. fewer FL rounds. The real FL benefit is **privacy**, not energy.

Ridge Point = Peak Compute / Peak Memory Bandwidth. Cortex-M4 at 168 MHz: peak compute ≈ 336 MFLOPS (single-cycle MAC = 2 FLOPS/cycle × 168 MHz). SRAM bandwidth: 32-bit bus at 168 MHz = 672 MB/s (single-cycle access, no wait states for on-chip SRAM). Ridge Point = 336 MFLOPS / 672 MB/s ≈ **0.5 Ops/Byte**. For comparison: H100 GPU ridge point ≈ 989 TFLOPS / 3,350 GB/s ≈ 295 Ops/Byte. The MCU's ridge point is ~590× lower than the H100's. This means MCU workloads become compute-bound only at very low arithmetic intensity (AI > 0.5). Most NN layers (conv, FC) have AI > 1.0, so they are actually **compute-bound** on MCUs — the opposite of GPUs where the same layers are often memory-bound. Element-wise ops (ReLU, add) with AI < 0.5 are memory-bound on both.
fixes.realistic_solution: A typical Cortex-M4 has a ridge point of approximately 0.5 Ops/Byte (336 MFLOPS / 672 MB/s). This is far lower than datacenter GPUs (~295 Ops/Byte for H100), but the implication is nuanced. A low ridge point means the system transitions from memory-bound to compute-bound at a lower arithmetic intensity. Many neural network layers (convolutions, fully-connected) have arithmetic intensity well above 0.5, making them **compute-bound** on MCUs. The real bottleneck is the absolute compute throughput (336 MFLOPS), not memory bandwidth. Element-wise operations (activation functions, residual adds) with AI < 0.5 are memory-bound.

fixes.napkin_math: Cortex-M4 peak performance: ~336 MFLOPS (168 MHz × 2 FLOPS/cycle). Model workload: 80 MFLOPs. Inference time = 80 / 336 = **238 ms**. Sliding window stride: 250 ms. 238 ms < 250 ms → **deadline met**, but only 12 ms slack (4.8%). This is dangerously tight. Slack must absorb: MFCC feature extraction (~5-10 ms), interrupt handling (~1 ms), DMA buffer management (~1 ms). With only 12 ms slack, any of these could push past the deadline. Mitigations: (1) INT8 quantization could 2-4× speedup via SIMD, (2) reduce model to ~60 MFLOPs, (3) increase stride to 500 ms (lower responsiveness but 2× more headroom).

**Activation memory (the real constraint):** Input: 48×48×64 × 1 byte (INT8) = 147,456 B = 144 KiB. Output: 48×48×128 × 1 = 294,912 B = 288 KiB. Peak SRAM for this layer (input + output live simultaneously): 144 + 288 = **432 KiB**. With depthwise separable: Step 1 (depthwise 3×3): input 48×48×64 = 144 KiB, output 48×48×64 = 144 KiB. Peak = 288 KiB. Step 2 (pointwise 1×1): input 48×48×64 = 144 KiB, output 48×48×128 = 288 KiB. Peak = 432 KiB. **Peak activation memory is identical at 432 KiB** — both exceed the 2 MB budget? No, 432 KiB fits in 2 MB. The scenario's premise is flawed: this layer's activations (432 KiB) fit easily in 2 MB SRAM. The real SRAM overflow would require a much larger layer or a much smaller MCU. **Parameter savings (affects Flash, not SRAM):** Standard: 3×3×64×128 = 73,728. Depthwise separable: (3×3×64) + (1×1×64×128) = 576 + 8,192 = 8,768. Reduction: 8.4×.
fixes.realistic_solution: The described layer's peak activation memory is ~432 KiB (input 144 KiB + output 288 KiB for INT8), which fits comfortably within the 2 MB SRAM of the Cortex-M7. There is no SRAM overflow to solve for this specific layer. A depthwise separable convolution reduces parameters by 8.4× (73,728 → 8,768) and FLOPs by ~8×, but does NOT reduce peak activation memory — the input and output tensor sizes remain identical. To actually reduce peak SRAM usage, you would need to: (1) reduce spatial resolution (add stride-2 or pooling before this layer), (2) reduce channel count, or (3) use in-place operations or operator fusion (e.g., TFLite Micro's memory planner can reuse buffers across non-overlapping lifetimes).

fixes.napkin_math: **1. Sequence Length (N):** N = (96 × 96) / (8 × 8) = 9,216 / 64 = **144 patches**. **2. Attention matrix size per head:** N × N = 144 × 144 = 20,736 elements. At FP32 (4 bytes): 20,736 × 4 = 82,944 B ≈ **81 KiB per head**. At INT8 (1 byte): 20,736 B ≈ **20.25 KiB per head**. **3. Multi-head attention:** With 4 heads (typical for tiny ViT): 4 × 81 KiB = 324 KiB (FP32) or 4 × 20.25 = 81 KiB (INT8). **4. Q, K, V matrices:** Each is N × d_head. With d_head=32 and 4 heads: 3 × 144 × 128 × 4 bytes = 221 KiB (FP32). **5. Total attention layer peak:** Attention matrices + Q/K/V ≈ 324 + 221 = **545 KiB (FP32)** for ONE layer. This single attention layer exceeds the CNN's entire 250K-parameter footprint in SRAM. With multiple layers, the ViT is completely infeasible on 1 MB SRAM. The CNN's peak activation (e.g., 48×48×32 = 72 KiB) is far smaller because convolutions process local patches, never materializing an N×N matrix.

**Standard 3×3 Conv2D (64 input, 128 output):** Weights: 3 × 3 × 64 × 128 = **73,728 parameters**. Bias: 128. Total: 73,856. At INT8: 73,856 bytes ≈ **72.1 KiB**. **Depthwise Separable Convolution:** Depthwise (3×3, 64 channels): 3 × 3 × 64 = 576 params + 64 bias = 640. Pointwise (1×1, 64→128): 1 × 1 × 64 × 128 = 8,192 params + 128 bias = 8,320. Total: 640 + 8,320 = **8,960 parameters**. At INT8: 8,960 bytes ≈ **8.75 KiB**. **Reduction: 73,856 / 8,960 ≈ 8.2×** fewer parameters. **FLOPs (assuming 25×25 spatial output):** Standard: 2 × 3 × 3 × 64 × 128 × 25 × 25 = 92.2 MFLOPs. Depthwise separable: DW = 2 × 3 × 3 × 64 × 625 = 0.72 M + PW = 2 × 64 × 128 × 625 = 10.2 M = **10.9 MFLOPs**. FLOP reduction: ~8.5×. Choose depthwise separable — 8× savings in both parameters and compute, critical for the 256 KB SRAM budget.

Consider a layer: 16×16 input, 96 input channels, 160 output channels, INT8. **Standard Conv (3×3):** Weights: 3×3×96×160 = 138,240 params = 135 KiB. Output activation: 16×16×160 = 40,960 B = 40 KiB. Peak SRAM (input + output + weights in cache): (16×16×96) + (16×16×160) + partial weights = 24.6 + 40 + ~10 = **~75 KiB**. **Depthwise Separable:** DW weights: 3×3×96 = 864 B. PW weights: 96×160 = 15,360 B. Total weights: 15.9 KiB (8.7× fewer). DW output: 16×16×96 = 24.6 KiB. PW output: 16×16×160 = 40 KiB. Peak SRAM (DW input + DW output): 24.6 + 24.6 = 49.2 KiB. Peak SRAM (PW input + PW output): 24.6 + 40 = 64.6 KiB. **Key insight:** A generic NAS might prefer the standard conv (fewer layers, simpler graph) while a hardware-aware NAS would prefer the depthwise separable (64.6 KiB peak vs. 75 KiB, and 8.7× fewer weights). With a 256 KB SRAM budget, this 10 KiB difference per layer compounds across the network. Hardware-aware NAS must include peak Tensor Arena size as a hard constraint, not just FLOPs.

A/B fleet = 1M × 10% = 100K devices. Assume: Model A = 200 KB (safe, proven). Model B = 450 KB (pushes flash limits). OTA data cost: $0.01/MB over cellular. Device replacement cost: $50/unit (including shipping + labor). **Data transfer cost:** Model B OTA: 100K × 0.45 MB × $0.01 = **$450**. **Bricking risk:** Model A (proven): 0.01% brick rate → 100K × 0.0001 = 10 bricks → 10 × $50 = $500. Model B (pushes flash): 0.5% brick rate (larger image = more failure modes during flash write) → 100K × 0.005 = 500 bricks → 500 × $50 = **$25,000**. **Total cost comparison:** Model A OTA: $200 data + $500 bricks = $700. Model B OTA: $450 data + $25,000 bricks = **$25,450**. The brick cost is 55× the data cost. A 0.5% failure rate — seemingly tiny — costs $25K on a 100K fleet. At full fleet (1M devices): 0.5% = 5,000 bricks = $250,000. **Lesson:** At fleet scale, reliability dominates TCO. Always A/B test OTA on <1% of fleet first, with automatic rollback on any brick signal.

Assume: 96×96 grayscale input, 8×8 patch size, 4 attention heads, d_model=128 (d_head=32), 4 layers, INT8 precision. **1. Sequence length:** N = (96/8)² = 144 patches. **2. Attention matrix per head:** 144 × 144 = 20,736 elements × 1 byte (INT8) = 20.25 KiB. **3. All heads:** 4 × 20.25 = 81 KiB for attention maps alone. **4. Q, K, V matrices (one layer):** 3 × 144 × 128 × 1 byte = 55.1 KiB. **5. Single attention layer peak:** 81 + 55.1 = **136.1 KiB**. **6. MLP block (typical 4× expansion):** 144 × 512 = 72 KiB intermediate. **7. One Transformer block peak:** ~136 + 72 = **208 KiB**. This single block already uses 208 / 512 = **40.6% of total SRAM** — and we haven't counted input embeddings, layer norms, or the classification head. With 4 layers (even with memory reuse), total peak will exceed 512 KB. The MobileNetV2 baseline uses only 220 KiB peak. **Verdict: ViT is infeasible.** Stick with CNN-based architectures (MobileNet, MCUNet) that avoid the O(N²) attention bottleneck.

fixes.realistic_solution: The memory overflow is caused by the first layer's large activation tensors, not its parameter count. A depthwise separable convolution reduces parameters and FLOPs but does NOT reduce activation memory — the input and output tensor dimensions remain unchanged. To fix the activation overflow, you must reduce the spatial dimensions (add stride-2 in the first layer) or reduce channel count. For example, if the first layer produces a 49×10×64 MFCC feature map output (INT8), that alone is 31.4 KiB. But if the input is raw audio (16,000 samples × 1 byte = 15.6 KiB) and the first conv expands to 64 channels at full resolution, the output is 16,000 × 64 = 1,000 KiB — exceeding the 256 KB budget. The fix: use stride-4 or stride-8 in the first layer to immediately downsample, reducing peak activation to 16,000/8 × 64 = 125 KiB.
fixes.napkin_math: **Parameter comparison (3×3 conv, 1→64 channels):** Standard: 3 × 3 × 1 × 64 = 576 params. DS: (3×3×1) + (1×1×1×64) = 9 + 64 = 73 params. Reduction: 7.9×. **But activation memory (the actual problem):** Assume first layer input: MFCC spectrogram 49 frames × 10 coefficients × 1 byte = 490 B. First layer output (64 filters, no stride): 49 × 10 × 64 = 31,360 B = 30.6 KiB. Peak SRAM (input + output): 0.49 + 30.6 = **31.1 KiB** — fits in 256 KB. If raw audio input (16,000 × 1 = 15.6 KiB) with 64-channel 1D conv, no stride: 16,000 × 64 = 1,000 KiB — **exceeds 256 KB by 4×!** Fix with stride-8: 2,000 × 64 = 125 KiB peak output. Total: 15.6 + 125 = 140.6 KiB — fits. **Key insight:** Depthwise separable convolution does NOT help here. The fix is architectural: stride or pooling to reduce activation dimensions.

**Service time (Ts):** Model = 80 MFLOPs. MCU = 960 MFLOPS. Ts = 80 / 960 = **83.3 ms per inference**. **Viability check:** The scenario omits the real-time deadline. Let's examine common cases: (a) If deadline = 100 ms (10 Hz vibration sampling): Ts = 83.3 ms < 100 ms → viable, but only 16.7 ms slack (16.7%). Utilization ρ = 83.3/100 = 83.3%. Queue will grow during burst arrivals. (b) If deadline = 50 ms (20 Hz sampling): Ts = 83.3 ms > 50 ms → **system is non-viable**. Events arrive faster than they can be processed. Queue grows without bound. Every event misses its deadline. (c) If deadline = 200 ms (5 Hz sampling): Ts = 83.3 ms << 200 ms → comfortable, ρ = 41.7%. **For case (b), fixes:** (1) INT8 quantization with SIMD: ~2-4× speedup → Ts = 21-42 ms (fits). (2) Prune model to 40 MFLOPs → Ts = 41.7 ms (fits). (3) Reduce sampling to 10 Hz if application allows.
fixes.realistic_solution: The diagnosis depends on the real-time deadline, which must be specified. The service time is Ts = 80 MFLOPs / 960 MFLOPS = 83.3 ms. If events arrive faster than every 83.3 ms, the system is fundamentally non-viable — the queue grows without bound and every event misses its deadline. Increasing queue size only delays the crash. The only valid solutions are: (a) reduce model FLOPs via quantization/pruning to bring Ts below the inter-arrival time, (b) use a faster MCU, or (c) reduce the sampling rate if the application permits.

fixes.napkin_math: For a 48×48 input, INT8: **Activation memory:** Standard conv output: 48 × 48 × 64 = 147,456 B = 144 KiB. Input: 48 × 48 × 32 = 73,728 B = 72 KiB. Peak (input + output): 72 + 144 = **216 KiB**. DS intermediate (after depthwise): 48 × 48 × 32 = 72 KiB. DS final output: 48 × 48 × 64 = 144 KiB. DS peak (intermediate + output, during pointwise): 72 + 144 = **216 KiB**. Activation memory is identical — no savings. **Weight memory:** Standard: 3 × 3 × 32 × 64 = 18,432 params = **18 KiB** (INT8). DS: depthwise (3×3×32 = 288) + pointwise (32×64 = 2,048) = 2,336 params = **2.3 KiB**. Savings: 7.9×. **MACs (48×48 spatial output):** Standard: 3 × 3 × 32 × 64 × 48 × 48 = 42.5M MACs. DS: depthwise (3×3×32×48×48 = 0.66M) + pointwise (32×64×48×48 = 4.7M) = **5.4M MACs**. Savings: 7.9×. **Verdict:** DS saves 7.9× in weights (18→2.3 KiB) and compute (42.5→5.4M MACs), but activation peak is unchanged at 216 KiB. On a 512 KB SRAM device, this leaves 296 KiB for other layers, DMA buffers, and firmware.

Cortex-M7 pipeline: 6 stages → misprediction penalty = ~6 cycles. With random data (50% positive, 50% negative), branch predictor misfires ~50% of the time. Per element: 50% × 1 cycle (correct) + 50% × (1 + 6) cycles (mispredict + flush) = 0.5 + 3.5 = **4.0 cycles/element average**. For 10,000 elements: 4.0 × 10,000 = 40,000 cycles expected. Scenario states 30,000 cycles = 3.0 cycles/element, suggesting ~33% mispredict rate (partially predictable data). Compared to branchless: 1 cycle/element × 10,000 = 10,000 cycles. **Slowdown: 3.0× (or 200% slower)** with the branchy version. Fix: use branchless select: `y[i] = x[i] * ((x[i] > 0) ? 1.0f : 0.1f)` or CMOV-style intrinsics. ARM SIMD (if available) can process 4 elements/cycle with no branching. Lesson: on pipelined MCUs, data-dependent branches in inner loops are performance killers.

**168 MHz:** Inference time = 60 ms at 50 mW. Assume 1 inf/s → sleep 940 ms. Sleep power: assume 10 µW. Energy/cycle = (50 mW × 60 ms) + (0.01 mW × 940 ms) = 3.0 mJ + 0.0094 mJ = **3.009 mJ**. **48 MHz:** Clock ratio: 168/48 = 3.5×. Inference time = 60 × 3.5 = 210 ms at 15 mW. Sleep: 790 ms at 10 µW. Energy/cycle = (15 mW × 210 ms) + (0.01 mW × 790 ms) = 3.15 mJ + 0.0079 mJ = **3.158 mJ**. **Comparison:** 48 MHz uses 5% more energy per inference cycle. **Daily energy:** 168 MHz: 3.009 mJ × 86,400 inf/day = 259.98 J/day = 72.2 mWh/day. 48 MHz: 3.158 mJ × 86,400 = 272.85 J/day = 75.8 mWh/day. **CR2032 (675 mWh) lifetime:** 168 MHz: 675 / 72.2 = **9.3 days**. 48 MHz: 675 / 75.8 = **8.9 days**. The faster clock wins by 0.4 days because race-to-sleep minimizes total energy despite higher instantaneous power.

**Battery:** 150 mAh × 3.7V = 555 mWh. **Predicted (spreadsheet):** Target 45 days → budget = 555 mWh / (45 × 24h) = 0.514 mW average = 139 µA at 3.7V. **Actual:** 15 days → actual = 555 mWh / (15 × 24h) = 1.54 mW = 416 µA at 3.7V. **Gap: 3.0× (416/139).** The spreadsheet likely only counted: MCU inference (1 inf/s × 0.5 mJ = 0.5 mW) + MCU sleep (10 µW) ≈ 0.51 mW → matches 139 µA prediction. **What the spreadsheet missed:** (1) IMU always-on: accelerometer at 100 Hz ≈ 0.2 mW. (2) LDO quiescent current: typical low-cost LDO Iq ≈ 50-100 µA × 3.7V ≈ 0.19-0.37 mW. (3) GPIO leakage on unused pins: ~10-50 µA total. (4) Voltage regulator efficiency loss: ~15% overhead. **Reconstructed budget:** MCU active: 0.5 mW. MCU sleep: 0.01 mW. IMU: 0.2 mW. LDO Iq: 0.3 mW. GPIO leakage: 0.15 mW. Regulator loss: 0.17 mW. **Total: ~1.33 mW ≈ 360 µA** — close to measured 416 µA. Remaining gap is board-level parasitics. **Lesson:** Always measure whole-board current, not just MCU. The MCU is often <40% of system power.
fixes.realistic_solution: The inference model was benchmarked correctly — the 3× gap comes from everything the power spreadsheet ignored: (1) always-on sensor current (IMU, ~0.2 mW), (2) LDO quiescent current (~0.3 mW), (3) GPIO leakage on unused/floating pins (~0.15 mW), and (4) voltage regulator efficiency losses (~15%). These 'invisible' loads are always present, even during sleep, and collectively exceed the MCU's own sleep current by 10-50×. The fix: measure whole-board current with a µA-resolution meter (e.g., Nordic PPK2), account for every component in the power budget, and configure all unused GPIOs as inputs with pull-downs.

The periodic self-test is blocking the main loop, causing the watchdog to fire. The CRC32 checksum over 256 KB of external QSPI flash is slow: QSPI typical read throughput is ~10-50 MB/s, so 256 KB takes 5-25 ms. But the real killer is that QSPI reads often disable interrupts or monopolize the bus, and if the self-test is implemented as a single blocking call, the main loop cannot run during this time. With a 40 ms main loop + 25 ms self-test = 65 ms total, this exceeds the 50 ms real-time deadline (causing missed sensor readings) and approaches the 100 ms watchdog timeout. If there is any additional jitter (cache miss, interrupt latency), the total exceeds 100 ms and the watchdog triggers a reboot. The fix: chunk the self-test into small incremental CRC operations (e.g., 4 KB per main loop iteration). Each chunk takes ~0.1-0.5 ms, negligible impact on the 40 ms loop. Full CRC completes over 64 iterations (64 × 50 ms = 3.2 s), which is perfectly acceptable for a periodic integrity check.
fixes.napkin_math: **QSPI flash read speed:** ~25 MB/s (typical quad-SPI at 50 MHz). **Blocking CRC time:** 256 KB / 25 MB/s = **10.2 ms** (best case). With CRC32 computation overhead: ~12-15 ms. **Timing analysis (worst case):** Main loop: 40 ms. Blocking self-test: 15 ms. Total: 55 ms > 50 ms deadline → **missed real-time deadline**. If self-test coincides with end of watchdog window: 40 ms (loop) + 15 ms (self-test) + 40 ms (next loop before pat) = 95 ms. Add any jitter (5+ ms): **>100 ms → watchdog fires**. **Chunked solution:** 256 KB / 4 KB chunks = 64 chunks. Per-chunk time: 4 KB / 25 MB/s + CRC compute ≈ 0.2 ms. Impact per main loop: 0.2 / 50 = 0.4% overhead. Full CRC completes in: 64 × 50 ms = **3.2 seconds** — acceptable for hourly integrity checks.

**Input histogram monitoring on Cortex-M7:** Image: 96×96 grayscale = 9,216 pixels. Histogram: 256 bins × 4 bytes (uint32) = **1 KiB SRAM**. Computation: one pass over 9,216 pixels = 9,216 increments ≈ 9,216 cycles at 1 cycle/increment. At 400 MHz Cortex-M7: 9,216 / 400M = **0.023 ms** — negligible vs. inference time (~10-50 ms). **Drift detection:** Store reference histogram (from training data): 256 × 4 = 1 KiB in flash. Compute mean and std of current frame vs. reference using chi-squared distance or simple mean-shift: mean = Σ(i × count[i]) / total_pixels. If |current_mean − reference_mean| > threshold (e.g., 15 on 0-255 scale), flag drift. **Telemetry:** Transmit 4 summary statistics (mean, std, min, max) = 16 bytes per reading, fits easily in 51-byte LoRaWAN packet alongside sensor data. **Total overhead:** 2 KiB SRAM (current + reference histograms), 0.023 ms compute, 16 bytes telemetry. Cost: essentially zero. **Detection capability:** Lens yellowing shifts the histogram mean downward (darker) and compresses the upper range. A 15% accuracy drop corresponds to a detectable mean shift of ~20-30 intensity levels.

**SRAM budget (256 KiB total):** 1. Firmware + stack: ~16 KiB. 2. DMA buffers (simultaneous acquisition required): Audio: 16 kHz × 2 bytes × 0.1s double-buffer = 2 × 3.2 KB = 6.4 KiB. Accel: 100 Hz × 6 bytes × 1s double-buffer = 2 × 0.6 KB = 1.2 KiB. Temp: negligible (<0.1 KiB). Total DMA: ~8 KiB. 3. Feature extraction workspaces: MFCC for audio: ~4 KiB (FFT scratch). Accel features: ~1 KiB. Total: ~5 KiB. 4. Tensor arenas — all three must be active simultaneously: Audio CNN: ~60 KiB (largest, MFCC input + conv activations). Accel CNN: ~20 KiB. Temp FC: ~2 KiB. Total arenas: **82 KiB**. 5. Fusion layer + app state: ~8 KiB. **Grand total: 16 + 8 + 5 + 82 + 8 = 119 KiB** — fits in 256 KiB with 137 KiB headroom. **Flash budget (512 KiB):** Audio model weights: ~40 KiB. Accel model weights: ~15 KiB. Temp model weights: ~2 KiB. Fusion classifier: ~4 KiB. Firmware code: ~100 KiB. Total: ~161 KiB — fits with 351 KiB to spare. **Key constraint:** All three tensor arenas must be allocated simultaneously (no sharing) since all pipelines run concurrently. The audio model's arena dominates — optimizing it (e.g., INT8, smaller architecture) directly increases headroom.
fixes.realistic_solution: Since all three sensor pipelines must be active simultaneously, we cannot use a shared/time-multiplexed tensor arena. Each model needs its own dedicated arena. The SRAM budget breaks into five categories: firmware/stack (16 KiB), DMA double-buffers for all three sensors (8 KiB), feature extraction scratch space (5 KiB), three independent tensor arenas (82 KiB total), and fusion/app state (8 KiB). Total: ~119 KiB, fitting within 256 KiB. The audio pipeline dominates both SRAM (60 KiB arena) and Flash (40 KiB weights). The key design decisions: (1) use INT8 quantization throughout to halve activation memory, (2) keep models small (DS-CNN for audio, tiny 1D-CNN for accel, 2-layer FC for temp), (3) fuse sensor outputs with a lightweight classifier rather than a complex multi-modal architecture.

Implement a three-layer on-device fault detection system within the Cortex-M4's constraints: **Layer 1 — Staleness detector (trivial cost):** Compare each new reading to the previous one. If identical for N consecutive readings (e.g., N=10, representing 10 hours), set a fault flag. Cost: 4 bytes SRAM (previous reading), 1 comparison per hour. **Layer 2 — Variance monitor:** Maintain a running variance over the last 24 readings (24 hours). Healthy air quality sensors show diurnal variation. If variance drops below a threshold (e.g., <0.1% of expected range), flag suspected sensor failure. Cost: 24 × 4 = 96 bytes SRAM for circular buffer, ~50 cycles of computation per hour. **Layer 3 — Telemetry encoding:** Encode a 1-byte health status in each 51-byte LoRaWAN packet (0=healthy, 1=stale, 2=low-variance, 3=out-of-range). The backend aggregates health flags across the fleet to identify the ~2% of failed devices for targeted replacement.
fixes.napkin_math: **On-device resource cost:** SRAM: Previous reading (4 B) + variance buffer (24 × 4 = 96 B) + health flag (1 B) = **101 bytes** — 0.04% of 256 KiB. Compute: 1 comparison + 1 running variance update per hour ≈ 100 cycles/hour. At 168 MHz, that is 100 / 168M = 0.0000006 s = **0.6 µs per hour** — negligible. **Telemetry cost:** 1 byte added to 51-byte packet → 50 bytes remaining for sensor data (98% preserved). **Fleet-level detection:** 100,000 devices × 1 health byte/hour × 24 hours = 2.4 MB/day for the entire fleet. Backend scans for devices with consecutive stale/low-variance flags. At 2% failure rate: ~2,000 devices flagged. With N=10 consecutive stale readings as threshold: detection latency = **10 hours** after sensor failure. False positive rate: extremely low (healthy sensors show natural variance from HVAC, occupancy, weather). **Targeted replacement cost:** 2,000 devices × ~$20/replacement = $40,000 vs. full fleet recall of 100,000 × $20 = $2M. Savings: **$1.96M** (98% cost reduction).
